title: 2024/09/12 - Petitions
displayTitle: 2024-09-24 - Petitions Committee - Petition of Deb Hayes: Investigate Midwifery Council’s removal of ‘woman & baby’ from Scope of Practice
topic: Petition of Deb Hayes: Investigate Midwifery Council’s removal of ‘woman & baby’ from Scope of Practice
description: 12 September 2024 | Petitions Committee Petition of Deb Hayes: Investigate Midwifery Council’s removal of ‘woman & baby’ from Scope of Practice Submitters: Midwifery Council, Lesley Clarke, CEO Kiley Clark, Council Co-chair Jessica Schreiber, Registrar Petition of Te Rito Maioha: Implement a 1:4 ratio for children under three years of age attending ECE Submitters: Te Rito Maioha: Cathy Wilson, Chief Executive, Montessori Aotearoa Rob McCann, Lead Communications Advisor Tui Lou Cassidy, Publications Manager, Childspace Lauren Stafford, Principal, Childspace Ministry of Education: John Brooker, General Manager, System, Connections & Early Learning Policy Graham Bussell, Senior Policy Manager, ECE System Investment Policy Paul Scholey, Senior Policy Manager, ECE System & Regulatory Policy Isabel Warden, Assistant Policy Analyst, ECE System Investment Policy Chris Jamieson, Principal Adviser, ECE Operational Policy Design
descriptionSummary: Petition of Deb Hayes: Investigate Midwifery Council’s removal of ‘woman & baby’ from Scope of Practice. Midwifery Council, Lesley Clarke, CEO. Kiley Clark, Council Co-chair

Um, so I'd just like to add in, uh, alongside with our submission that the intent of the council has always been to improve equitable health outcomes and maternity for whanau, um, Maori and Pacifica.

Um, it has been a transformational process to ensure a culturally and clinically safe scope of practice reflects improvements to maternity care in communities in, um, and I can actually attest to it as a working LMC in the community, um, on ground level working with whanau. Um, what that actually translate into, um, out there doing the vein, uh, the perinatal mortality morbidity report 16th report release this year, again, continues to challenge the maternity sector to acknowledge persistent and systemic health inequities.

Um, therefore it has mandated, um, high order Act 2022 and Tata Health Strategy have mandated the health sector's responsibility to ensure that we strive for a strong focus on health equity in the midwifery sector and midwives responsibility to embed into practice.

Tata has tasked the health sector to be collaborative, innovative, flexible, and create diverse solutions within practice. The process that we undertook to review the scope of practice was in keeping with this a collaborative reference group inclusive of UA t incorporating framework.

So we know that one of the key matters that has been raised through this process is the use of the word whanau. We would like to express that whanau was adopted into the scope of middle midwifery practice and was accepted by the council with the intent of being inclusive and all encompassing. It is a kapu that could meet the needs of wau supporting the profession to embed into practice with the te Maori worldview and reduce health inequities and improve maternity outcomes for whanau.

While the contemporary use of whanau reflects, reflects family, the presence of whanau in the revised scope provides a contextual and layered meaning, which adds depth and representation of more than family from an English definition. And I would also just like to clarify that we did not redefine the word whanau.

Um, we refer to it, um, as a mechanism being reflective of what whanau looks like out there in the community, um, for women wpu pregnant person and baby. So despite misinterpretation of the revised scope, the council's intention remains that the primary obligation of a midwife is to wpu woman, pregnant person and newborn baby.

The inclusion of whanau is to recognize the importance of whanau in creating a safe, nurturing environment. Um, just, just a final couple of comments.

Um, the other thing to note is that scopes of practice are, um, secondary legislation and therefore within the remit of the Regulations Review Committee. 10 minutes and uh, hopefully we can time questions. Excellent. So, on behalf of Meha and Child Space, we are here to speak to the petition to implement a one to four ratio for under threes. We are here today because children can't advocate for themselves. Children do not get a say but deserve more than the care and attention being afforded by the current legislation standards.

That current standard is the ratio of teachers to children that was arbitrarily set in the 1960s and then again in the 1990s without any evidence or research. The current ratio of teachers to children in ECE are one teacher to five children under two years, and two teachers for 20 children over the age of two.

What that means is that as soon as a child turns two, there can be 22 year olds super being supervised, supported and cared for with only two teachers. My colleague too will talk, were more about what that looks like in practice and how unworkable that is. The ratios are also incompatible with science, which acknowledges that a human being is an infant until they reach the age of three years.

Yet our current model creates an arbitrary cutoff at two years that leaves two to 3-year-old children is the lowest funded group. Yet these children have all the needs of under twos children, for example, nappy changing, developing social skills and developing language.

So what we have is an outdated minimum ratio that is unsafe and that is the government that continues to use when setting budgets and is the basis of the minimum standard in ECE. Now, we should note that despite the additional cost to ECE services, many high quality providers have already put in place lower children to teacher ratios and they fund this through parental fees or reduced margins.

Not all providers can afford to do this. And that brings us back to what we're asking parliament to do, which is to draw a line in the sand and commit to a lower ratio and ensure that all children in ECE have a better and safer baseline of service. In fact, that commitment was in the National Party education policy, and I quote, national will progressively improve the adult to child ratio for under two year olds in early childhood education ratios are not just about safety and quality issue for our tamari, they are one

of the building blocks of ECE. So when we get it wrong, any changes to ECE policy in funding can cause a range of unintended consequences. Budget 2023 was an example of that. Let's look at the rationale for lower ratios. Lower ratios are beneficial for children, not just because they're safer, but they enable teachers to focus on children.

They better support child staff relationships. They increase the quality of interactions between adults and children and they reduce the stress on tamaki. If we are not enabling that kind of interaction, I've just listed, we are not providing quality ECE, which is what is required for children to develop to their full potential.

Before I hand you over to my colleague Tui, I'd like to conclude with this. When tamari are young, they need dedicated attention to stay safe and have their needs tended to. But it's much more than that quality. ECE requires qualified teachers to develop child staff relationships because the research is clear.

The quality of interactions between adults and children play a hugely important role in stimulating early learning. And as parents, if you are putting your children into ECE, you want them to be cared for and educated because they are our most precious and treasured gifts. Ki Thank you Kelly.

My name's til again, nice to see you guys. even blowing your nose, things like that. You'll need my help. And almost half of you, based on the stats, will have some kind of additional physical, emotional or cognitive need. Ratios are the foundation upon which ECE regulation and funding are built. They set how much time and the quality of relationships between a teacher and children.
